content:
# Exodus Chapter 29 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~50v–51r (PDF pp. 99–101); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 71–73 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_029_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_029_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_029_vaticanus_page_003.jpg`

---

## The Consecration of the Priests (vv. 1–9)

**1** "And this [**vezeh**] is the thing [**haddavar**] which [**asher**] you shall do [**ta'aseh**] to them [**lahem**] to consecrate [**leqaddesh**] them [**otam**] to minister as priests [**lekhaheno**] to Me [**li**]: Take [**qach**] one [**echad**] young bull [**par**], a son of [**ben**] the herd [**baqar**], and two [**ve'eylim**] rams [**shneyyim**] without blemish [**temimim**],

**2** and bread of [**velechem**] unleavened bread [**matstsot**] and unleavened cakes [**vechalot matstsot**] mixed [**belulot**] with oil [**bashamen**], and unleavened wafers [**urqiqey matstsot**] smeared [**meshuchim**] with oil [**bashamen**]; of fine wheat flour [**soleth chittim**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**].

**3** And you shall put [**venatattha**] them [**otam**] into [**al**] one [**sal echad**] basket [**sal**], and you shall bring them near [**vehiqravta**] them [**otam**] in the basket [**bassal**], along with [**ve'et**] the bull [**happar**] and the two [**shney**] rams [**ha'eylim**].

**4** And Aaron [**ve'et-Aharon**] and his sons [**ve'et-banav**] you shall bring near [**taqriv**] to [**el**] the entrance of [**petach**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**], and you shall wash [**verachatzta**] them [**otam**] with water [**bammayim**].

**5** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] the garments [**et-habbegedim**], and you shall clothe [**vehilbashta**] Aaron [**et-Aharon**] with the tunic [**et-hakketonet**] and the robe of [**ve'et-me'il**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] and the ephod [**ve'et-ha'efod**] and the breastpiece [**ve'et-hachoshen**]; and you shall gird [**ve'afadeta**] him [**lo**] with the skillfully woven band of [**becheshev**] the ephod [**ha'efod**].

**6** And you shall set [**vesamta**] the turban [**hammitsnefet**] upon [**al**] his head [**rosho**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] the crown of [**et-nezer**] holiness [**haqqodesh**] upon [**al**] the turban [**hammitsnafet**].

**7** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] the oil of [**et-shemen**] anointing [**hammishchah**], and you shall pour it [**veyatsaqta**] upon [**al**] his head [**rosho**], and you shall anoint [**umashachta**] him [**oto**].

**8** And his sons [**ve'et-banav**] you shall bring near [**taqriv**], and you shall clothe [**vehilbashta**] them [**otam**] with tunics [**kuttonot**].

**9** And you shall gird [**vechagharta**] them [**otam**] with sashes [**avnet**] — Aaron [**Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] — and you shall bind [**vechaavta**] caps [**migba'ot**] on them [**lahem**]; and the priesthood [**vehay'tah lahem kehunnah**] shall be [**lechuqqat**] theirs as a statute [**olam**] forever; and you shall ordain [**umille'ta**] the hand of [**yad**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and the hand of [**veyad**] his sons [**banav**].

---

## The Bull of the Sin Offering (vv. 10–14)

**10** And you shall bring near [**vehiqravta**] the bull [**et-happar**] before [**lifney**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**], and Aaron [**vesamakh Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] shall lay [**et-yedeyhem**] their hands [**al**] upon [**rosh**] the head of [**happar**] the bull.

**11** And you shall slaughter [**veshachatta**] the bull [**et-happar**] before [**lifney**] YHWH, at the entrance of [**petach**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**].

**12** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] of the blood of [**middam**] the bull [**happar**], and you shall put it [**venatattha**] on [**al**] the horns of [**qarnot**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] with your finger [**be'etsba'ekha**], and all [**ve'et-kol**] the remaining blood [**haddam**] you shall pour out [**tishpokh**] at [**el**] the base of [**yesod**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**].

**13** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] all [**et-kol**] the fat [**hachelev**] that covers [**hamekhasseh**] the entrails [**et-haqqerev**], and the lobe [**ve'et hayyoteret**] upon [**al**] the liver [**hakkaved**], and the two [**ve'et-shtey**] kidneys [**hakkelayot**] and the fat [**ve'et-hachelev**] that [**asher**] is upon them [**aleyhen**], and you shall turn them into smoke [**vehiqtarta**] upon the altar [**hammizbechah**].

**14** But the flesh of [**ve'et-besar**] the bull [**happar**] and its skin [**ve'et-oro**] and its dung [**ve'et-pirsho**] you shall burn [**tisrof**] with fire [**ba'esh**] outside [**michutz**] the camp [**lammachaneh**]; it is [**hu**] a sin offering [**chattat**].

---

## The First Ram — The Burnt Offering (vv. 15–18)

**15** And the one [**ha'echad**] ram [**ha'ayil**] you shall take [**tiqqach**], and Aaron [**vesamakh Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] shall lay [**et-yedeyhem**] their hands [**al**] upon [**rosh**] the head of [**ha'ayil**] the ram.

**16** And you shall slaughter [**veshachatta**] the ram [**et-ha'ayil**], and you shall take [**velaqachta**] its blood [**et-damo**] and you shall dash it [**vezaraqta**] against [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] all around [**saviv**].

**17** And the ram [**ve'et-ha'ayil**] you shall cut [**tenatteyach**] into its pieces [**lintachav**], and you shall wash [**verachatzta**] its entrails [**qirbo**] and its legs [**ukhera'av**], and you shall put them [**venatattha**] upon [**al**] its pieces [**netachav**] and upon [**ve'al**] its head [**rosho**].

**18** And you shall turn into smoke [**vehiqtarta**] the whole [**et-kol**] ram [**ha'ayil**] upon the altar [**hammizbechah**]; it is [**hu**] a burnt offering [**olah**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**], a pleasing aroma [**reyach nichoach**], an offering by fire [**ishsheh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

---

## The Second Ram — The Ram of Ordination (vv. 19–28)

**19** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] the second [**hasheni**] ram [**ha'ayil**], and Aaron [**vesamakh Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] shall lay [**et-yedeyhem**] their hands [**al**] upon [**rosh**] the head of [**ha'ayil**] the ram.

**20** And you shall slaughter [**veshachatta**] the ram [**et-ha'ayil**], and you shall take [**velaqachta**] of its blood [**middamo**] and you shall put it [**venatattha**] on [**al**] the lobe of [**tenukh**] the right [**yemeen**] ear of [**ozen**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and on [**ve'al**] the lobe of [**tenukh**] the right [**yemanit**] ear of [**ozen**] his sons [**banav**], and on [**ve'al**] the thumb of [**bohen**] their right [**yadam hayyemanit**] hand [**ve'al**] and on [**bohen**] the great toe of [**raglam**] their right [**hayyemanit**] foot; and you shall dash [**vezaraqta**] the blood [**et-haddam**] against [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] all around [**saviv**].

**21** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] of [**min**] the blood [**haddam**] that [**asher**] is upon [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] and of [**umishemen**] the oil of [**hammishchah**] anointing, and you shall sprinkle [**vehizzeytha**] upon [**al**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and upon [**ve'al**] his garments [**begadav**] and upon [**ve'al**] his sons [**banav**] and upon [**ve'al**] the garments of [**bigdey**] his sons [**banav**] with him [**itto**]; and he shall be consecrated [**veqadash**], he [**hu**] and his garments [**ubegadav**] and his sons [**uvanav**] and the garments of [**ubigdey**] his sons [**banav**] with him [**itto**].

**22** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] from [**min**] the ram [**ha'ayil**] the fat [**hachelev**] and the fat tail [**veha'alyah**] and the fat [**ve'et-hachelev**] that covers [**hamekhasseh**] the entrails [**et-haqqerev**], and the lobe of [**ve'et yoteret**] the liver [**hakkaved**], and the two [**ve'et-shtey**] kidneys [**hakkelayot**] and the fat [**ve'et-hachelev**] that [**asher**] is upon them [**aleyhen**], and the right [**ve'et-shoq**] thigh [**hayyamin**] — for it [**ki**] is a ram of [**eyl**] ordination [**millu'im**] it [**hu**] —

**23** and one [**ve'echad**] round loaf [**kikkar**] of bread [**lechem**] and one [**ve'echad**] cake of [**challat**] oil-bread [**lechem shemen**] and one [**veraqiq echad**] wafer [**missal**] from the basket of [**hammatstsot**] unleavened bread [**asher**] that [**lifney**] is before [**YHWH**] YHWH.

**24** And you shall place [**vesamta**] all [**hakkol**] upon [**al**] the palms of [**kappey**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and upon [**ve'al**] the palms of [**kappey**] his sons [**banav**], and you shall wave [**vehenafta**] them [**otam**] as a wave offering [**tenufah**] before [**lifney**] YHWH.

**25** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] them [**otam**] from [**miyyadam**] their hands, and you shall turn them into smoke [**vehiqtarta**] on the altar [**hammizbechah**] upon [**al**] the burnt offering [**ha'olah**] as a pleasing aroma [**lereyach nichoach**] before [**lifney**] YHWH; it is [**hu**] an offering by fire [**ishsheh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**26** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] the breast [**et-hechazeh**] from the ram of [**me'eyl**] ordination [**hammillu'im**] that [**asher**] is for Aaron [**le'Aharon**], and you shall wave [**vehenafta**] it [**oto**] as a wave offering [**tenufah**] before [**lifney**] YHWH; and it shall be [**vehayah**] your portion [**lekha lemanah**].

**27** And you shall consecrate [**veqiddashta**] the breast of [**et chazeh**] the wave offering [**hattenufah**] and the thigh of [**ve'et-shoq**] the contribution [**hatterumah**] which [**asher**] was waved [**hunaf**] and which [**va'asher**] was raised [**huram**] from the ram of [**me'eyl**] ordination [**hammillu'im**], from that which [**me'asher**] is for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] and from that which [**ume'asher**] is for his sons [**levanav**].

**28** And it shall be [**vehayah**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] and for his sons [**ulevanav**] as a perpetual [**le choq olam**] statute [**me'et**] from [**beney**] the Sons of Israel [**Yisra'el**]; for [**ki**] it is [**hu**] a contribution [**terumah**], and a contribution [**uterumah**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] from [**me'et**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], from their peace offerings [**mizzivchey shalmeyhem**], their contribution [**terumatam**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

---

## The Sacred Garments Passed On (vv. 29–30)

**29** And the garments of [**uvigdey**] holiness [**haqqodesh**] that [**asher**] are for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] shall be [**yihyu**] for his sons [**levanav**] after him [**acharav**], to be anointed [**lehimmasheyach**] in them [**bahem**] and to be ordained [**ulemalle**] in them [**et-yadam**] their hand [**bahem**].

**30** Seven [**shiv'at**] days [**yamim**] the priest [**hakkohen**] from among his sons who succeeds him [**tachtav mibbanav**] shall wear them [**yilbasham**], he who [**asher**] comes [**yavo**] to [**el**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] to minister [**leshareth**] in the Holy Place [**baqqodesh**].

---

## Eating the Ordination Flesh (vv. 31–34)

**31** And the ram of [**ve'et eyl**] ordination [**hammillu'im**] you shall take [**tiqqach**], and you shall boil [**ubishaltta**] its flesh [**et-besaro**] in a holy [**bimqom qadosh**] place.

**32** And Aaron [**ve'akhal Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] shall eat [**et-besar**] the flesh of [**ha'ayil**] the ram and the bread [**ve'et-hallechem**] that [**asher**] is in the basket [**bassal**], at the entrance of [**petach**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**].

**33** And they shall eat [**ve'akhlu**] them [**otam**] — those things by which [**asher**] atonement was made [**kuppar**] by them [**bahem**] to ordain [**lemalle**] their hand [**et-yadam**] to consecrate [**leqaddesh**] them [**otam**]; and a stranger [**vezar**] shall not [**lo**] eat [**yokhal**] them, for [**ki**] they are holy [**qodesh hem**].

**34** And if [**ve'im**] any of the flesh of [**mibbasar**] the ordination [**hammillu'im**] or of [**umin**] the bread [**hallechem**] remains [**yivvater**] until [**ad**] the morning [**habboqer**], then you shall burn [**vesarafta**] the remainder [**et-hannotar**] with fire [**ba'esh**]; it shall not [**lo**] be eaten [**ye'akhel**], for [**ki**] it is holy [**qodesh hu**].

---

## Seven Days of Ordination (vv. 35–37)

**35** And you shall do [**ve'asita**] thus [**kakhah**] to Aaron [**le'Aharon**] and to his sons [**ulevanav**], according to all [**kekhol**] that [**asher**] I have commanded [**tsivvithi**] you [**otakh**]; seven [**shiv'at**] days [**yamim**] you shall ordain [**temalle**] their hand [**yadam**].

**36** And a bull [**upar**] of sin offering [**chattat**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] each day [**layyom**] upon [**al**] the atonements [**hakkippurim**]; and you shall purify from sin [**vechitta'ta**] upon [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] by making atonement [**bekhapperekha**] upon it [**alav**], and you shall anoint [**umashachta**] it [**oto**] to consecrate it [**leqaddesho**].

**37** Seven [**shiv'at**] days [**yamim**] you shall make atonement [**tekapper**] upon [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] and consecrate [**veqiddashta**] it [**oto**]; and the altar [**vehayah hammizbeyach**] shall be [**qodesh**] most holy [**qodashim**]; everything [**kol**] that touches [**hannogea**] the altar [**bammizbeyach**] shall be holy [**yiqdash**].

---

## The Daily Offerings — The Tamid (vv. 38–42)

**38** And this [**vezeh**] is what [**asher**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] upon [**al**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**]: lambs [**kevasim**], sons of [**beney**] a year [**shanah**], two [**shnayim**] each day [**layyom**], continually [**tamid**].

**39** The one [**ha'echad**] lamb [**et-hakkeves**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] in the morning [**babboqer**], and the second [**hasheni**] lamb [**et-hakkeves**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] between [**beyn**] the two evenings [**ha'arbayim**].

**40** And a tenth [**ve'issaron**] measure of fine flour [**solet**] mixed [**balul**] with oil of [**beshemen**] beaten olives [**katit**], a quarter [**revi'it**] of a hin [**hahhin**]; and a drink offering [**venesekh**] of a quarter [**revi'it**] of a hin [**hahhin**] of wine [**yayin**] for the one [**la**] lamb [**hakkeves ha'echad**].

**41** And the second [**hasheni**] lamb [**ve'et hakkeves**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] between [**beyn**] the two evenings [**ha'arbayim**]; like the grain offering of [**keminchath**] the morning [**habboqer**] and like its drink offering [**ukhenisskah**] you shall offer [**ta'aseh**] for it [**lah**], as a pleasing aroma [**lereyach nichoach**], an offering by fire [**ishsheh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**42** A continual [**tamid**] burnt offering [**olat**] for your generations [**ledorotheyekhem**], at the entrance of [**petach**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] before [**lifney**] YHWH, where [**asher**] I will meet [**ivvaded**] you [**lakhem**] there [**shammah**], to speak [**ledabber**] to you [**eleykha**] there [**sham**].

---

## The Promise of YHWH's Dwelling (vv. 43–46)

**43** And I will meet [**veno'adeti**] there [**shammah**] with the Sons of Israel [**livney Yisra'el**], and it shall be consecrated [**veniqdash**] by My glory [**bikhvodi**].

**44** And I will consecrate [**veqiddashti**] the Tent of [**et-ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] and the altar [**ve'et-hammizbeyach**]; and Aaron [**ve'et-Aharon**] and his sons [**ve'et-banav**] I will consecrate [**aqaddesh**] to minister as priests [**lekhaheno**] to Me [**li**].

**45** And I will dwell [**veshakhanti**] in the midst of [**betokh**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], and I will be [**vehayiti**] their God [**lahem le'Elohim**].

**46** And they shall know [**veyad'u**] that [**ki**] I [**ani**] am YHWH their God [**Eloheyhem**] who [**asher**] brought them out [**hotseti**] them [**otam**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**] to dwell [**leshakhni**] in their midst [**betokham**]; I [**ani**] am YHWH their God [**Eloheyhem**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**millu'im** (vv. 9, 22, 26, 27, 31, 33, 34) -- "ordination" or "installation," literally "fillings." The noun millu'im (plural of millu', from m-l-', "to fill") is the technical term for priestly consecration. It derives from the idiom mille' et-yad, "to fill the hand" (v. 9, 35), which designates the act of placing sacrificial portions into the hands of the one being ordained (see v. 24, where the offerings are placed upon the palms of Aaron and his sons). The "filling" is both literal -- the priest's hands are physically filled with the offering -- and symbolic: the priest receives from God, through Moses, the authority and substance of his office. He does not come to the priesthood empty-handed or self-appointed; his hands are filled by another. The seven-day duration of the ordination (vv. 35, 37) connects it to the creation week, suggesting that priestly ordination is a kind of new creation, a re-ordering of human life for divine service. The LXX renders millu'im as teleiōsis ("completion, perfection, consecration"), the noun from which the Epistle to the Hebrews draws its vocabulary of priestly "perfection" (Heb 7:11, teleiōsis), arguing that the Levitical priesthood could not bring teleiōsis and therefore required a new priestly order.

**chattat** (vv. 14, 36) -- "sin offering" or "purification offering." The noun chattat (from ch-t-', "to miss, sin, purify") designates the sacrifice whose primary purpose is to purge the sanctuary and the person of ritual contamination caused by sin. The bull of the sin offering in the ordination ceremony serves to purify the altar itself (v. 36), recognizing that even the instruments of worship must be cleansed before they can mediate between the holy God and sinful humanity. The burning of the bull's flesh, skin, and dung "outside the camp" (michutz lammachaneh, v. 14) is a distinctive feature of the chattat that signals the removal of impurity from the sacred precincts. The author of Hebrews makes explicit typological use of this detail: "Jesus also suffered outside the gate" (Heb 13:12), connecting Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem to the sin offering burned outside the camp. The LXX renders chattat as peri hamartias ("concerning sin") or hamartia ("sin [offering]"), a rendering that Paul echoes in Romans 8:3 (peri hamartias, "concerning sin" / "as a sin offering").

**olah** (vv. 18, 25, 42) -- "burnt offering" or "ascension offering." The noun olah (from '-l-h, "to go up, ascend") designates the sacrifice that is entirely consumed on the altar, ascending wholly to God as smoke. Unlike the chattat (sin offering) or the shelamim (peace offering), from which portions are eaten by priests or worshippers, the olah belongs entirely to YHWH -- nothing is retained by the offerer. The first ram of the ordination ceremony (vv. 15-18) is designated an olah, establishing at the very inauguration of the priesthood the principle of total self-offering. The description of the olah as a "pleasing aroma" (reyach nichoach, v. 18) uses anthropomorphic language rooted in the ancient Near Eastern concept of divine pleasure in sacrifice, but in the biblical context it signifies YHWH's gracious acceptance of the offering. The LXX renders olah as holokautōma ("whole burnt offering"), from which the English "holocaust" derives, and reyach nichoach as osmē euōdias ("aroma of fragrance"), the same phrase Paul applies to Christ's self-offering in Ephesians 5:2.

**tenufah** (vv. 24, 26, 27) -- "wave offering." The noun tenufah (from n-w-f, "to wave, swing, brandish") designates the ritual gesture of moving the offering in a horizontal motion, traditionally understood as waving toward the altar and back. In the ordination ceremony, the wave offering has a unique character: the fat portions, the right thigh, and the bread are placed upon the open palms of Aaron and his sons (v. 24), and then Moses waves them before YHWH. This is the definitive act of millu'im, the "filling of the hand" -- the priests hold the offering and it is waved before God, signifying that what fills the priestly hand is not personal possession but divine gift temporarily entrusted. The breast of the wave offering (chazeh hattenufah) and the thigh of the contribution (shoq hatterumah) become perpetual priestly portions (vv. 27-28), establishing the material support of the priesthood as arising from the sacrificial system itself. The LXX renders tenufah as epithema or afairema, and in some places as epōmis ("elevation"), reflecting interpretive variation in the Greek tradition regarding the nature of the ritual gesture.

**tamid** (vv. 38, 42) -- "continually, perpetually." The adverb tamid (from a root meaning "to stretch, extend") designates what is ongoing, regular, and uninterrupted. When applied to the daily burnt offering (olat tamid, v. 42), it specifies the foundational rhythm of Israelite worship: two lambs per day, one in the morning and one "between the two evenings" (beyn ha'arbayim, vv. 39, 41), without cessation. The tamid offering establishes that the priestly ministry is not occasional or episodic but continuous -- YHWH's altar must never be cold, His worship never silent. The tamid became so central to Israel's liturgical identity that its disruption under Antiochus IV Epiphanes (Dan 8:11-13; 11:31; 12:11) was regarded as the supreme act of sacrilege, and its restoration under the Maccabees (1 Macc 4:52-56) as the great act of covenant renewal. The LXX renders tamid as endelachismos or dia pantos ("through all [time]"), emphasizing the unbroken continuity of the offering.

### Grammatical Notes

**ivvaded lakhem shammah** (v. 42) -- "I will meet you there." The verb ivvaded is the Niphal first person singular of y-'-d ("to appoint, designate, meet by appointment"). The Niphal form here carries a reflexive-reciprocal nuance: "I will appoint Myself to meet you" or "I will keep My appointment with you." This is not a chance encounter but a covenantal rendezvous -- YHWH binds Himself to appear at the designated place (the entrance of the Tent of Meeting) at the designated time (the daily offerings). The verb y-'-d is the root of mo'ed ("appointed time, meeting"), ohel mo'ed ("Tent of Meeting"), and 'edut ("testimony") -- a constellation of terms that saturates the Tabernacle narrative. The preposition lakhem ("to you," plural) in v. 42a shifts to eleykha ("to you," singular) in v. 42b, a shift from addressing the Israelite community corporately to addressing Moses individually: YHWH will meet the people at the Tent, but He will speak to Moses there. The LXX renders ivvaded as entaxomai or gnōsthēsomai ("I will be known / I will make Myself known"), interpreting the divine meeting as an act of self-revelation.

**veno'adeti shammah livney Yisra'el** (v. 43) -- "And I will meet there with the Sons of Israel." The verb veno'adeti is again the Niphal of y-'-d, but here with the preposition li- rather than lakhem of v. 42. The slight shift is significant: in v. 42, YHWH meets "for you" (lakhem, dative of advantage); in v. 43, YHWH meets "with/for the Sons of Israel" (livney Yisra'el), broadening the scope from the priestly entourage to the entire covenant people. The result of this meeting is consecration: "it shall be consecrated by My glory" (veniqdash bikhvodi). The Niphal of q-d-sh ("to be consecrated") with the instrumental preposition be- ("by means of") indicates that the Tent is not sanctified by human ritual alone but by the kavod ("glory, weight, presence") of YHWH Himself. The glory is the active agent of consecration; human ritual merely prepares the space that divine presence fills. The LXX renders veniqdash bikhvodi as hagiasthēsetai en doxē mou ("it shall be sanctified in/by my glory").

**lekhaheno li** (v. 1, 44) -- "to minister as priest to Me." This phrase, which opened chapter 28 and there occurred four times, recurs here as a framing inclusion for the entire ordination sequence. The Piel infinitive construct of k-h-n with the dative li anchors the priesthood in service to YHWH, not to the people or the institution. The repetition in v. 44, where YHWH Himself is the speaker ("I will consecrate... to minister as priest to Me"), transforms the imperative of v. 1 (Moses commanded to consecrate) into a divine declaration of purpose: ultimately, it is YHWH who consecrates the priests for His own service. The LXX renders this consistently as hierateuein moi.

**beyn ha'arbayim** (vv. 39, 41) -- "between the two evenings." This temporal expression designates the time of the afternoon tamid offering. Its precise meaning was debated in antiquity: the Pharisaic tradition understood it as the period between the beginning of the sun's decline (roughly noon or early afternoon) and sunset, while the Samaritan and Sadducean traditions took it as the interval between sunset and full darkness (twilight). The expression uses the dual form of erev ("evening"), literally "between the two evenings," suggesting a liminal period -- a threshold in the day. This same phrase designates the time for slaughtering the Passover lamb (Exod 12:6), linking the daily tamid to the foundational act of redemption. The LXX renders beyn ha'arbayim as to pros hesperas ("toward evening") or deilēs ("in the afternoon").

### Structural Notes

Exodus 29 is the ritual complement to the sartorial instructions of chapter 28. If chapter 28 answered "What shall the priests wear?", chapter 29 answers "How shall the priests be made?" The chapter moves through a carefully ordered sequence: preparation (vv. 1-3), washing and vesting (vv. 4-9), the three sacrifices of ordination (vv. 10-28), provisions for succession and the sacred meal (vv. 29-34), the seven-day framework (vv. 35-37), the perpetual daily offering (vv. 38-42), and the climactic divine promise (vv. 43-46).

The three sacrifices form the structural and theological center of the chapter. The bull as chattat (sin offering, vv. 10-14) purifies the altar and the priests from sin; the first ram as olah (burnt offering, vv. 15-18) signifies total consecration to YHWH; and the second ram as eyl millu'im (ram of ordination, vv. 19-28) effects the actual ordination through the application of blood to the extremities of the priests' bodies and the filling of their hands with sacrificial portions. This threefold sacrificial sequence -- purification, consecration, ordination -- establishes the pattern that will govern Leviticus 8, where the ceremony is actually performed, and provides the template for all subsequent priestly service.

The application of blood to the right ear, right thumb, and right great toe of the priests (v. 20) is one of the most symbolically charged rituals in the Torah. The ear signifies hearing (obedience to the divine word), the hand signifies doing (performance of priestly duties), and the foot signifies walking (the conduct of priestly life). Blood on these three extremities consecrates the whole person -- perception, action, and direction -- to YHWH's service. The same ritual appears in the cleansing of the leper (Lev 14:14, 17, 25, 28), suggesting a deep connection between ordination and purification: the priest is, in a sense, a "cleansed" person, one whose whole body has been reclaimed from the realm of death and impurity for divine service.

The chapter's climax is not ritual but relational. After all the detailed instructions about sacrifices, blood, and offerings, the text culminates in YHWH's first-person declaration of intent: "I will dwell in the midst of the Sons of Israel, and I will be their God" (v. 45). This is the covenant formula (cf. Gen 17:7-8; Lev 26:12; Jer 31:33; Rev 21:3), the ultimate purpose of the entire Tabernacle project. The sacrificial system, the priesthood, the garments, the altar -- all exist to make possible the divine indwelling. The verb shakhanti ("I will dwell," from sh-k-n, "to dwell, tabernacle") is the root of mishkan ("Tabernacle, dwelling place") and of the later rabbinic term Shekhinah ("the divine presence"). YHWH's dwelling is not static but relational: "I will be their God" -- the priesthood mediates not a system but a relationship.

Verse 46 provides the theological basis for the entire Exodus narrative: the purpose of the exodus from Egypt was not merely political liberation but cultic communion -- "who brought them out from the land of Egypt to dwell in their midst." The exodus was for the sake of the indwelling. Freedom from Pharaoh was freedom for YHWH's presence. This retrospective reframing of the exodus event in terms of divine habitation connects the narrative arc of Exodus 1-18 (liberation) with Exodus 25-40 (Tabernacle construction) and reveals them as a single theological movement from bondage to communion.

### Theological Notes

The ordination ceremony of Exodus 29 establishes the theological architecture of biblical priesthood through three interrelated movements: purification (chattat), consecration (olah), and ordination (millu'im).

The sin offering (vv. 10-14) confronts the paradox at the heart of the priestly vocation: the one who is to mediate between God and humanity is himself a sinner. Before Aaron can offer sacrifices for others, sacrifice must be offered for him. The bull is slaughtered, its blood applied to the altar's horns, and its flesh burned outside the camp -- the fullest form of the sin offering, indicating that the contamination being addressed is severe. The altar itself must be purified (v. 36) before it can serve as the place of atonement, establishing the principle that the instruments of mediation require the same grace as the persons they serve. The author of Hebrews makes this the distinguishing point between the Levitical and the Christological priesthood: "He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for His own sins and then for those of the people" (Heb 7:27).

The burnt offering (vv. 15-18) enacts the principle of total consecration. The entire ram ascends to YHWH as smoke; nothing is returned to the offerer. This sacrifice, positioned between the sin offering and the ordination offering, declares that purified persons must then give themselves wholly to God before they can receive the authority to serve. The sequence -- cleansed, then offered, then commissioned -- anticipates the New Testament pattern: "present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God" (Rom 12:1), followed by the exercise of diverse gifts (Rom 12:3-8).

The ram of ordination (vv. 19-28) is unique in the sacrificial system. Its blood is applied not only to the altar but to the bodies of the priests -- their ears, hands, and feet. This somatic theology -- theology inscribed on the body -- declares that priesthood is not merely a role but a physical transformation. The blood that atones marks the very flesh of the one who will mediate atonement for others. The subsequent "filling of the hand" (v. 24) completes the ordination: the priest's hand, marked with sacrificial blood, is then filled with sacrificial offerings. What enters the blood-marked hand is the substance of the priestly vocation -- the offerings that the priest will present to God on behalf of the people for the rest of his life.

The daily tamid offering (vv. 38-42) extends the priestly consecration into perpetuity. The two daily lambs create the liturgical heartbeat of Israel's worship -- morning and evening, without interruption. The tamid is the rhythm within which all other sacrifices occur; it is the constant backdrop against which the festivals, the Day of Atonement, and the occasional offerings are set. YHWH's promise to "meet" the people at the time of the tamid (v. 42) transforms the daily sacrifice from obligation to rendezvous: the tamid is not merely Israel's offering to God but God's appointment with Israel.

The final verses (43-46) reveal the telos of the entire sacrificial-priestly system: divine indwelling. The phrase "I will dwell in the midst of the Sons of Israel" (veshakhanti betokh beney Yisra'el, v. 45) is the culmination not only of the ordination chapter but of the entire Tabernacle section (chs. 25-29). Everything described in these five chapters -- the ark, the table, the lampstand, the curtains, the boards, the altar, the court, the garments, the sacrifices -- exists to create the conditions under which the holy God can dwell among His people. The closing declaration "I am YHWH their God" (ani YHWH Eloheyhem, v. 46) is the covenant formula in its most concentrated form, and its repetition at the chapter's end seals the ordination instructions with the ultimate assurance: the God who commands this elaborate system of mediation is the same God who has already acted to redeem His people ("who brought them out from the land of Egypt") and whose purpose in redemption is communion ("to dwell in their midst"). The entire priesthood, with all its complexity, exists to serve this single end: that YHWH and Israel might dwell together.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 29 (pages 71-73) presents the LXX rendering with close correspondence to the MT in structure and content, though with characteristic interpretive features. At v. 1, the LXX renders leqaddesh otam as hagiasai autous ("to consecrate them"), using the standard Greek equivalent for the Hebrew q-d-sh. At v. 9, the ordination phrase mille'ta yad is rendered teleiōseis tas cheiras ("you shall perfect the hands"), maintaining the LXX's consistent use of teleioō for millu'im. At v. 14, the sin offering is rendered peri hamartias estin ("it is concerning sin"), the same phrase later taken up by Paul in Romans 8:3. At v. 18, the burnt offering is described as holokautōma kuriō ("whole burnt offering to the Lord") with osmē euōdias ("aroma of fragrance"). At v. 20, the application of blood to the ear lobe, thumb, and great toe follows the MT closely: epi ton lochon tou ōtos tou dexiou Aarōn ("upon the lobe of the right ear of Aaron"). The Vaticanus text at vv. 22-25 preserves the ordination ram instructions with the distinctive term teleiōsis for the ordination rite. At v. 35, the seven-day ordination period is rendered hepta hēmeras teleiōseis tas cheiras autōn ("seven days you shall perfect their hands"). At vv. 38-42, the daily tamid offering is rendered with dia pantos ("continually") for tamid, and the temporal phrase beyn ha'arbayim as to pros hesperas ("toward evening"). The chapter's climax in vv. 43-46 is rendered with particular force in the LXX: v. 45 reads kai kataskēnōsō en meso huiōn Israēl kai esomai autōn theos ("and I will tabernacle in the midst of the sons of Israel and I will be their God"), using kataskēnoō, the verb cognate with skēnē ("tent, tabernacle"), which John 1:14 echoes in its declaration that the Word "tabernacled" (eskēnōsen) among us. At v. 46, the LXX renders the exodus purpose clause as tou kataskēnōsai en autois ("to tabernacle among them"), making explicit the Tabernacle theology: the exodus was for the sake of the in-dwelling. The Vaticanus manuscript shows the chapter spanning from the middle of page 71 (beginning after the end of ch. 28) through page 72 and onto page 73, where chapter 30 (marked KA in the LXX section numbering) begins in the lower portion of the right column.

--- LOGOS
